title: Urban Voids Grounds for Change
ocr_text: Phildelphia is the tragic American City. Likemany American cities, people diffused fromthe city in times of economic and social change. What remains is largely homogenous: a landscape of fragmented rowhouse blocks amongst empty lots. Businesses and amenities are limited. Well-known parks are far away. Landscape differentiation, a sense identity, is lacking. We think of land no longer needed as emptiness. We think of density lost and voids. Can we cast our perceptions aside? Philadelphia is writhe not with voids, but with unharnessed open space - the commodity other cities struggle to maintain and to create. Philadelphia is rooted in William Penn's vision: living with enough land to sustain community and well being. Philadelphia is the American urban dream. A landscape for reclaimed identities is proposed. Several distinct “palettes”, mixes of select plantings and simple hardscapes, are developed for strong characteristic colors, scales, and bloom. Vacant lots are cleared and grouped in

ocr_text: New Urban BIOCK - theehiaseiphia project Community Greens Inspired by the sense of green throughout the city whereby one is never far fom a river stream, or larger ecosystem, "we purpose the creation of community greens’ surrounded by revitalized urban blocks. The urban glue these spaces provide, link the city physically and metaphorically, and seek to engender a sense of community by evitalizing areas ‘of decline. ‘Ourresearch explored the quotidian of block occupancy. informing the design of a newly integrated steet system that remains active throughout the day by combining residential use with shops restaurants, and cultura venues. Surrounding the greens, we propose granting ownership/lease to residents and create opportunity for new development within the community. Using the micro-scale of individual city blocks, the green spaces create a framework within the city that ‘connects to larger natural environments unique to Philadelphia, including the Schuykill and Delaware Rivers,and

title: Urban Voids Grounds for Change

textBlocks.text: UNCOVER / RECOVER 0579 Millions of dollars are buried beneath Philadelphia's vacant lots. A rich network of city infrastructure lies dormant beneath a layer of dirt and refuse. We propose the uncovering and reutlization of these hidden resources, ee INFRASTRUCTURAL FOUNDATIONS saa Bis eae Th Fa I a" Vacant lots, once domestic or private sites, are appropriated for public use. Their foundations retain al 1 nl zat an index of their former lives and preserve the scale and fabric of the urban block. These remnants ety a a are the sites of intervention, allowing programs to be divided into localized and maintainable units. rae ek a The existing infrastructure of underground utilities allows for a diverse set of programs that redefine id sel oes at 4 traditional public space. a H ig dy 7 a a ae iid TACTICAL INTERVENTION i Egil joy en La rat h i Philadelphia faces a type of open space distinctly different from traditional, planned public space. WR a as ia in J Urban voids are profuse and dispersed, occurring in areas of relatively low urban density. Our Se ee ' WA ml proposal inserts local, condensed zones of activity within a larger field of landscape, wilderness or a 1 met i fe te non-programmed open space. Programs are scaled to the size and needs of the community and Ny KS oe nl thereby engender a sense of ownership and community involvement. fi: nd oe ay re A 7.) iin hi. Ler all ee PLACEHOLDERS Ae bas dl ur proposal provides a solution to Philadelphia's urban voids but does not accept this as a Peg permanent and pervasive condition. By preserving the lot lines, our intervention promotes the Ln el i Ai gall regeneration of residential neighborhoods, and inhibits the encroachment of suburban enclaves ie ty Seyi: i Rm Tt he to which cafes Te of aa enw by ian lll au a: 4 wa i Treen Unsan Conomon, Profuse and Dispersed Square.
